questions.question: According to research, what percentage of helpers experience compassion fatigue without proper boundaries?

options.text: About 25% experience compassion fatigue
About 40% experience compassion fatigue
About 56% experience compassion fatigue without proper boundaries
Nearly all (95%+) helpers experience compassion fatigue

questions.explanation: Research shows 56% of helpers experience compassion fatigue without proper boundaries. However, the encouraging news is that 82% recover with early intervention and support systems. This emphasizes the importance of recognizing warning signs early, establishing clear boundaries, seeking peer support, engaging in personal therapy, and maintaining self-care practices. Compassion fatigue is predictable and preventable, not inevitable.

questions.question: What is the optimal number of intensive mentoring relationships for sustainable practice according to research?

options.text: 1-2 relationships to avoid any burnout risk
3-4 intensive relationships for optimal sustainability
8-10 relationships to maximize impact
As many as possible—more is always better

questions.explanation: Research suggests 3-4 active mentees/intensive relationships is optimal for sustainable practice. Helpers maintaining 3-4 deep helping relationships report lowest burnout rates and highest satisfaction. Below 3 feels low-impact; above 4-5 creates overwhelm. This is specifically for intensive 1-on-1 relationships, not group work or light-touch support. Deep mentoring requires emotional presence, processing time, and mental space—you can't do that for 10+ people without depletion.

questions.question: What are the three types of helper depletion described in the lesson?

options.text: Physical burnout, emotional burnout, and compassion fatigue
Mental burnout, spiritual burnout, and relational burnout
Primary burnout, secondary burnout, and tertiary burnout
Acute burnout, chronic burnout, and terminal burnout

questions.explanation: The three types are: (1) Physical burnout—chronic fatigue, illness, exhaustion from too many hours and insufficient self-care, (2) Emotional burnout—feeling numb, cynicism, difficulty empathizing from absorbing others' pain without processing your own, and (3) Compassion fatigue—reduced empathy, feeling helpless, vicarious trauma from exposure to others' suffering without adequate processing. Each requires different recovery approaches, though they often overlap.

questions.question: According to the 'Oxygen Mask Principle,' why is self-care essential for helpers?

options.text: Because helpers are more important than the people they serve
Because you can't serve from depletion—self-care enables sustained service, not selfishness
Because self-care is a luxury reward you earn after helping enough people
Because helpers deserve more care than non-helpers

questions.explanation: The Oxygen Mask Principle states: Put on your oxygen mask BEFORE helping others—not because you matter more, but because you can't help if you're unconscious. Self-care follows the same logic: You can't serve from depletion. Taking care of yourself IS taking care of your capacity to serve. Research shows helpers who prioritize wellbeing serve longer and more effectively. Self-care serves your mission, making it strategic rather than selfish.

questions.question: If you're already experiencing mid-to-late stage burnout, what is the first step in recovery?

options.text: Push through with more willpower and determination
Immediately reduce helping hours or take complete break—stop the bleeding
Add more clients to increase your sense of purpose
Just wait it out—burnout resolves on its own over time

questions.explanation: The first step is 'Stop the Bleeding'—immediately reduce helping hours or take complete break. You cannot recover while continuing the same pace that created burnout. Burnout doesn't improve through willpower—only through actual rest and boundary change. After that: (2) Get professional support (therapy), (3) Physical recovery (sleep, nutrition, medical care), (4) Reconnect with meaning, (5) Rebuild with boundaries before returning. Research shows 82% recovery rate with proper support and intervention.

title: Managing Your Energy and Avoiding Burnout - Quiz
